content.full_text:
ATKINSON · FARASYN
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

PAUL B. SMITH
EDWARD L. ARNOLD
HAROLD S. TOPPEL
ROBERT K. BOOTH, JR.
STEVEN G. BAIRD

660 WEST DANA STREET
P.O. BOX 279
MOUNTAIN VIEW, CALIFORNIA 94042

J. M. ATKINSON (1892-1962)
L. M. FARASYN (1915-1979)

TELEPHONE (415) 967-6941
FACSIMILE (415) 967-1395

April 9, 1992

Ms. Blanca Breeze
Staff Attorney
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for conflict of interest opinion
City of East Palo Alto

Dear Ms. Breeze:

This letter is a follow-up to our recent telephone discussions concerning various conflict of interest questions arising from the employment of a city council member by a non-profit organization owing real property within a designated redevelopment area. Although you kindly provided telephonic advice at that time, we would like to obtain a written opinion on behalf of the FPPC, and for this purpose, I will attempt to outline the facts in more detail and set forth a list of particular actions for which we would request a determination as to whether the councilmember will be disqualified from participation.

The East Palo Alto Councilmember in question, Ms. Nevida Butler, is employed as a full time salaried staff employee of a non-profit corporation which has qualified for tax exemption status under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. The main activity of the corporation is distribution of food and clothing to homeless and indigent persons in and around the City of East Palo Alto. The organization is managed by a 17 member Board of Directors, all of whom are volunteers. Ms. Butler is not a member of the Board, nor does she have any ownership, proprietary, or other interest in the organization beyond her position as a salaried employee. The non-profit corporation has gross annual receipts of approximately $300,000.00, virtually all of which is derived from contributions from members of the public at large and institutional charities.

The members of the East Palo Alto City Council also serve as the members of the East Palo Alto Redevelopment Agency. As of the time Ms. Butler was first elected to the Council, the City and Redevelopment Agency had established the University Circle Redevelopment Project Area, adopted General Plan amendments and a Specific Plan for the Project Area, rezoned the Project Area to a classification consistent with the General and Specific Plans, entered into a Disposition and Development Agreement with a private developer for acquisition and development of the Project Area as a mixed use office complex, and granted a Planned Unit Development (PUD) permit and tentative subdivision map approval to the developer.

Ms. Bianca Breeze
April 9, 1992
Page Two


The Council and Agency also certified a program EIR and a project EIR relating to the
foregoing legislative actions and development approvals. The original project was
thereafter modified as a result of litigation commenced by neighboring cities objecting
to certain environmental impacts and these modifications were approved by the East
Palo Alto Planning Commission. Consequently, at this point in time, all development
approvals for the modified project have been granted. The next stage of activity
would be the acquisition of the Project Area by the Redevelopment Agency (through
negotiation and eminent domain proceedings), relocation of the existing occupants,
transfer of the land to the developer, abandonment of existing public streets within
the Project Area, and commencement of construction.

The non-profit corporation owns real property within the University Circle
Redevelopment Project Area. This parcel represents 1 out of a total of 37 parcels of
land to be acquired by the Agency. The non-profit organization intends to continue its
activities at a new location within the immediate area. Ms. Butler will continue to be
employed throughout this process. The corporation will be entitled to relocation
benefits provided by law, in addition to compensation for the fair market value of its
real property.

Although project approvals have already been granted, further actions by
the City Council and the Redevelopment Agency will be required for implementation
of such approvals and performance under the existing Disposition and Development
Agreement between the developer and the Agency. Moreover, it is possible that
further changes will be made to the land use and design for a portion of the project.
Our questions with respect to these actions would include the following:

A.    ACQUISITION OF REAL PROPERTY BY THE AGENCY

     In order to acquire each parcel of land within the Project Area, the
Redevelopment Agency will authorize the initiation of negotiations with the
respective property owners (commencing with the presentation to each individual
owner of an offer to purchase, based upon the appraised value of such owner's
property). If a negotiated purchase is not accomplished, the Agency will need to adopt
a Resolution of Necessity for the conduct of eminent domain proceedings.

     Based upon our telephone conversation, it would appear that Ms. Butler
should not participate in Council or Agency actions directly related to the acquisition
of the specific real property owned by the non-profit corporation. We would
appropriate your confirmation of this ruling. However, since the acquisition of each
parcel in the Project Area is a separate transaction (and a separate legal
proceeding, if required), we believe that Ms. Butler should not be disqualified from
participating in actions relating to such other acquisitions. The Agency has a legal
obligation to pay the fair market value for each parcel, as determined by independent
appraisal. Because the various parcels within the Project Area have different uses,
sizes, physical improvements and adjustments to value, such as contamination from
toxic materials, the amount paid for one parcel does not necessarily have an economic
effect upon the amount paid for another parcel. Thus, even though it may be the case
that Ms. Butler is disqualified from participating in decisions relating to the

Ms. Blanca Breeze
April 9, 1992
Page Three


acquisition of her employer's property, we would request your concurrence with the
opinion that she is not disqualified from participating in decisions regarding acquisition
of other properties within the Project Area in which her employer has no ownership
interest.

B.  RELOCATION OF EXISTING OCCUPANTS

     The acquisition and development of the University Circle Project Area will
require relocation of approximately 58 commercial establishments and the occupants
of approximately 73 residential units. The non-profit corporation which employs
Councilmember Butler has been included in the group of "commercial establishments"
for purposes of determining relocation benefits.

     The Redevelopment Agency is required by law to adopt a relocation plan
establishing benefits and procedures for occupants of the Project Area who will be
displaced by the development. The nature and amount of financial and other
relocation benefits will be governed by statutory provisions set forth in the
Government Code and guidelines adopted thereunder by the State Department of
Housing and Community Development. In this case, since a portion of the Project
Area will be transferred to Caltrans for freeway purposes, the federal relocation
guidelines will also be applicable.

     As in the case of property acquisition, we would assume that Ms. Butler
cannot participate in any decisions relating to the specific relocation benefits that
may be payable to her employer. We would appreciate your confirmation of this
opinion. Also, we would raise the further question as to whether Ms. Butler can
participate in the decision to adopt or amend the general relocation plan. It should be
noted that such plan contains dollar amounts and descriptions of moving costs and
expenses that are payable to businesses and non-profit organizations that are forced to
relocate. However, these amounts and descriptions are derived from the state and
federal law and guidelines. The benefits are intended to pay or reimburse the
occupant for the cost of the relocation so as to avoid any net loss (i.e. increase in
expenses) resulting from the displacement. Moreover, the general rules apply equally
to all of the occupants of the Project Area. Although the Agency has authority to
increase such benefits and to resolve disputes and make interpretations concerning
entitlements, these matters would be handled on a case by case basis, thus giving Ms.
Butler the opportunity to abstain if a decision was needed with respect to benefits
claimed by her employer. Similarly, we feel Ms. Butler should be able to participate in
any case by case decisions concerning benefits claimed by residential or commercial
occupants other than her own employer because such decisions have no economic
effect upon her employer.

C.  MODIFICATION OF PROJECT LAND USES AND DESIGN

     The approved development project includes two parcels to be improved with
high-rise office structures and a third parcel to be improved with a hotel. There
is a question as to whether a hotel is currently feasible and it is likely that some form
of alternative proposal will be considered for this parcel. It is also possible that some

Ms. Blanca Breeze
April 9, 1992
Page Four


design and land use changes could be made to either of the office parcels. For
example, instead of a hotel, there might be a mixed use development consisting of
retail establishments and residential units. This type of change would require
amendment to the specific plan for the Project Area and corresponding amendments to
the PUD permit and development agreements. Since these changes have no economic
effect upon the acquisition of the property owned by Ms. Butler's employer, or the
relocation benefits payable to this non-profit corporation, we believe Ms. Butler should
not be disqualified from participating in any decisions concerning such project
modifications. We would request your concurrence with this opinion.

D.     MISCELLANEOUS DECISIONS

     Implementation of the approved project may involve various miscellaneous
decisions, including the following:

     1.     Granting or modification of agreements with project consultants.
            For example, the Agency has existing contracts with an acquisition
            consultant, a relocation consultant, special legal counsel, and a
            planning consultant.

     2.     Cooperative agreements with Caltrans for the reconstruction of a
            freeway interchange, which is included as part of the development
            project.

     3.     Professional service agreement with the County of San Mateo for the
            performance of services on behalf of Caltrans for acquisition of
            right-of-way.

     4.     Cooperative agreements with other public agencies and utilities
            pertaining to the construction of public improvements required to
            service the development or mitigate impacts from the project.

     We do not believe that any of the implementing actions and agreements, such as those
listed above, would have an economic effect upon the non-profit corporation that
employs Ms. Butler. We would request your concurrence with this opinion.


     To summarize the foregoing, we have raised the following questions:

     (1)    Is Ms. Butler disqualified from participating in decisions relating to
            the acquisition by the Redevelopment Agency of property owned by
            her employer?

     (2)    Whether or not Ms. Butler is disqualified under Question 1 above, can
            she participate in decisions relating to the acquisition of other
            parcels within the Project Area not owned by her employer?

Ms. Blanca Breeze
April 9, 1992
Page Five


(3)  Can Ms. Butler participate in decisions to adopt or amend the general
     relocation plan pertaining to the entire Project Area?

(4)  Whether or not Ms. Butler is disqualified under Question 3 above, can
     she participate in decisions regarding case by case determinations on
     relocation benefits claimed by occupants of the Project Area (other
     than her own employer), based upon the provisions of the adopted
     relocation plan?

(5)  Can Ms. Butler participate in decisions regarding modifications to the
     approved development project, such as changes in land use and
     structure design?

(6)  Can Ms. Butler participate in decisions concerning implementation
     and administration of the approved development project, such as
     approval of agreements with consultants and other agencies.

If there is any additional information you require in connection with this
request, please do not hesitate to contact me by telephone.  Your prompt response
would be greatly appreciated.


                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        HAROLD S. TOPPEL
                                        East Palo Alto City Attorney

sections.question:
A. ACQUISITION OF REAL PROPERTY BY THE AGENCY

In order to acquire each parcel of land within the project Area, the Redevelopment Agency will authorize the initiation of negotiations with the respective property owners (commencing with the presentation to each individual owner of an offer to purchase, based upon the appraised value of such owner's property). If a negotiated purchase is not accomplished, the Agency will need to adopt a Resolution of Necessity for the conduct of eminent domain proceedings.

B. RELOCATION OF EXISTING OCCUPANTS

The acquisition and development of the University Circle Project Area will require relocation of approximately 58 commercial establishments and the occupants of approximately 73 residential units.

C. MODIFICATION OF PROJECT LAND USES AND DESIGN

The approved development project includes two parcels to be improved with high-rise office structures and a third parcel to be improved with a hotel.

D. MISCELLANEOUS DECISIONS

Implementation of the approved project may involve various miscellaneous decisions, including consulting agreements, cooperative agreements with Caltrans, and professional service agreements.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether City Council member Nevida Butler, who is employed by a non-profit organization owning property within a redevelopment project area, is disqualified from participating in various City Council and Redevelopment Agency decisions regarding property acquisition, relocation benefits, project modifications, and implementing agreements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. The document contains detailed facts and a numbered list of specific questions but no CONCLUSION or FPPC advice. The final five summarized questions appear to be truncated at the end of the provided text.

C. MODIFICATION OF PROJECT LAND USES AND DESIGN

D. MISCELLANEOUS DECISIONS

Implementation of the approved project may involve various miscellaneous decisions, including consulting agreements, cooperative agreements with Caltrans, and professional service agreements.

embedding.summary: An attorney representing the City of East Palo Alto requests a written FPPC conflict of interest opinion regarding whether Council member Nevida Butler must recuse herself from decisions involving a University Circle Redevelopment Project, given her employment by a non-profit organization that owns property within the project area.
